hero.tagline: I design and build data foundations that turn trusted analytics into intelligent, autonomous systems.

background.label: Professional Background
background.story: Started as a data analyst — learning what real business questions look like when they arrive as a Slack message at 9am. One thing became obvious quickly: analysis is rarely the bottleneck. The data infrastructure underneath almost always is. I began rebuilding it — writing the pipelines, modeling the logic, automating the reports. The shift from analyst to platform engineer wasn't planned. It was the natural answer to a problem that kept repeating.

steps.title: Analytics Engineering
steps.note: dbt · semantic layers · data modeling · governance

steps.title: AI-Native Systems
steps.note: LLM reasoning · autonomous agents · decision intelligence

principles.statement: Reliable analytics starts with reliable data.
principles.sub: Every AI system is only as trustworthy as the data foundations beneath it.
The goal isn't reporting. It's removing the human from the feedback loop entirely.
LLMs don't fix bad data. They amplify it. Build the foundation first.

learning.label: Currently Learning
learning.intro: Deepening expertise in the next layer of the stack:

vision.label: Vision
vision.heading:
Building the data stack
for AI-native intelligence.
vision.text: The next generation of analytics systems won't just visualise data — they'll reason over it, act on it, and close the feedback loop autonomously. I'm building toward that future: AI-native systems that combine governed data platforms, semantic layers, and autonomous AI agents into a single, coherent stack.

caseStudy.inProgressSub: Engineering documentation and architecture diagrams are being prepared for this project.

overview: Gaming clubs generate significant operational data — session bookings, equipment usage, payment transactions, and subscription lifecycle events. The challenge: all of this lived in disconnected systems, with no unified view for management or any agreed-upon metric definitions.

This project built an end-to-end analytics platform: automated nightly ingestion from operational sources into a central PostgreSQL data warehouse, dbt transformation layers with consistent metric definitions enforced as code, and a Metabase reporting layer that delivers trustworthy business metrics daily.

problem.context: Three operational systems produced conflicting revenue figures. Weekly reports were assembled manually from CSV exports. There was no agreed definition of core metrics, making strategic decisions harder and slower than necessary.
challenges: Operational data distributed across three disconnected systems with no shared identifiers
Revenue figures differed between systems due to inconsistent calculation logic
Weekly reporting assembled manually from CSV exports — slow and error-prone
No single authoritative definition of core business metrics
No visibility into cross-system patterns, such as subscription vs. session revenue mix

architecture.description: A layered data platform built around a central PostgreSQL data warehouse, with Airflow orchestrating nightly ingestion and dbt owning all transformation logic.

layers.label: Operational Systems
layers.detail: POS · Booking System · CRM · Subscription Service

layers.label: Data Ingestion
layers.detail: Airflow DAGs · Python extractors · nightly schedule

layers.label: dbt Models
layers.detail: Business logic · metric definitions · data contracts

layers.label: Analytics Layer
layers.detail: Aggregated KPIs · revenue attribution · cohort metrics

streams.name: Payments
streams.desc: Transaction records extracted nightly, reconciled against session data, and classified by payment method and type.

streams.name: Gaming Sessions
streams.desc: Session start/end events with equipment allocation, duration tracking, and hourly utilization metrics.

streams.name: Subscriptions
streams.desc: Activation, renewal, and expiration events with revenue attribution and churn signal tracking.

streams.name: CRM Data
streams.desc: Customer profile updates, segment classification, and acquisition channel attribution.

streams.name: Operational Metrics
streams.desc: Hourly capacity utilization, peak period detection, and equipment availability signals.

results: Automated nightly pipeline replaced manual weekly CSV reporting cycle
Consistent metric definitions enforced across all dbt models and downstream dashboards
Single management dashboard covering revenue, sessions, and subscriptions
Data discrepancies between systems identified and resolved through reconciliation logic
Reporting cycle shortened from several days to overnight

lessons.title: Data quality precedes modeling
lessons.body: Source system inconsistencies required more attention than the pipeline itself. Invest in profiling and reconciliation before writing transformation logic — bad source data doesn't get fixed by good SQL.

lessons.title: Metrics are a business conversation
lessons.body: Defining what 'revenue' or 'active session' means requires stakeholder alignment, not just technical decisions. Contested definitions produce contested dashboards and eroded trust.

lessons.title: Automation compounds in value
lessons.body: Each additional data source added disproportionate value once the ingestion pattern was established. Pipeline infrastructure is a multiplier — the second source costs a fraction of the first.

lessons.title: Early mart delivery accelerates feedback
lessons.body: Delivering mart-layer tables early gave stakeholders something concrete to react to, which improved requirements quality for subsequent iterations considerably.

dwhModeling.description: The warehouse follows a three-layer medallion architecture enforced through dbt project structure. Each layer has a distinct contract: raw data is never modified, staging is where cleaning happens, marts are where business logic lives.

layers.name: Raw Layer
layers.description: Exact copies of source system data. Insert-only, no transformations, full history preserved. Serves as an audit log and re-processing baseline.

models.name: raw_pos_transactions
models.description: Direct copy of POS transaction records with original field names and types

models.name: raw_bookings
models.description: Session booking events exactly as received from the booking system API

models.name: raw_subscriptions
models.description: Subscription lifecycle events: activations, renewals, cancellations, expirations

models.name: raw_crm_profiles
models.description: Customer profile snapshots from CRM with full field history

layers.name: Staging Layer
layers.description: Cleaned, typed, and deduplicated source data. One staging model per source table. Business logic is explicitly forbidden at this layer.

models.name: stg_payments
models.description: Typed payment records with nulls handled, currency normalised, duplicates removed

models.name: stg_sessions
models.description: Normalised session events with duration computed, equipment IDs standardised

models.name: stg_subscriptions
models.description: Unified subscription events with consistent status codes and date types

models.name: stg_customers
models.description: Merged customer profiles with segment tags and acquisition channel attribution

layers.name: Mart Layer
layers.description: Business-facing tables optimised for reporting queries. Aggregated KPIs, revenue attribution logic, and cohort metrics all live here.

models.name: mart_revenue
models.description: Daily and monthly revenue broken down by category, payment method, and source system

models.name: mart_sessions
models.description: Hourly utilisation metrics, equipment allocation rates, peak period flags

models.name: mart_subscriptions
models.description: Active subscriber counts, churn rates, renewal pipeline, and MRR tracking

models.name: mart_customers
models.description: Customer segments, LTV estimates, acquisition cohorts, and engagement scores

streams.name: Executive Daily Summary
streams.desc: Single-page management dashboard covering yesterday's revenue, active sessions, and subscriber count versus prior periods. Delivered as a scheduled Metabase email at 8am.

streams.name: Revenue Analytics
streams.desc: Payment method mix, revenue breakdown by category (sessions vs. subscriptions vs. merchandise), trend lines, and daily deviation from weekly averages.

streams.name: Session Utilisation
streams.desc: Equipment allocation by hour-of-day, seat utilisation heatmap, peak period identification, and capacity headroom analysis for operational planning.

streams.name: Subscription Health
streams.desc: Active subscriber counts, renewal pipeline for the next 30 days, churn signals from declining engagement, and MRR trend with cohort breakdown.

futureImprovements.title: ClickHouse Migration
futureImprovements.description: Move analytical query workloads from PostgreSQL to ClickHouse. Sub-second aggregations on large time-series datasets will unlock real-time operational dashboards that PostgreSQL cannot serve at scale.

futureImprovements.title: Real-time Streaming Pipeline
futureImprovements.description: Add a Kafka-based streaming layer alongside the nightly batch pipeline. Priority data streams: payment events and session open/close events. Enables live utilisation monitoring and instant revenue visibility.

futureImprovements.title: AI Analytics Layer
futureImprovements.description: Expose the dbt semantic layer via a natural language interface powered by Claude. Management self-service queries — 'what drove revenue growth last week?' — without writing SQL or relying on predefined reports.

futureImprovements.title: Semantic Layer API
futureImprovements.description: Formalise metric definitions as a programmatic API using dbt metrics or Cube.dev. Enables consistent metric consumption across Metabase, future AI agents, and any downstream application.

labs.label: Analytics Labs
labs.heading: The Lab
labs.description: Experiments in autonomous analytics, semantic data models, and AI-native reporting.

items.title: Semantic SQL Layer
items.description: Teaching LLMs to query DWHs through a structured semantic layer instead of raw SQL.
items.longDescription: LLMs are remarkably good at reasoning over structured data — when given the right abstraction. This experiment builds a semantic layer that maps business concepts to SQL constructs, allowing Claude to generate reliable, governed queries without hallucinating column names.

items.title: Autonomous Report Agent
items.description: Agent that monitors KPI deviations and generates executive summaries without human prompting.
items.longDescription: A LangChain agent that runs on a schedule, queries the DWH for KPI deviations, decides if they're significant, and writes an executive summary with root-cause hypotheses. No human in the loop.

items.title: Vector DWH Index
items.description: Embedding business metrics into vector space for similarity search and anomaly clustering.
items.longDescription: What if you could find similar business situations from history without writing a query? This experiment embeds aggregated metrics as vectors in ClickHouse, enabling semantic similarity search across time periods, cohorts, and segments.
